text: as she scrolls through the videos. And though her strength is all but depleted, her eyes widen at the terrifying footage. With every camera angle open and playing on the screen, she realizes how many times Frankie had gone under the house, before and after Biscuit’s escape. She watches him heal the kitten after the coyotes got him. She watches him heal her leg after her drunken tumble down the stairs. She even watches him heal house-cats she hadn’t even realized had gotten hurt. But as captivating as it is, she finds herself focusing on the shadows instead. They’ve been healing too. He’s been healing. The first time she sees Charlie in the background of a video he’s little more than a tangle of crawling bones. But with each passing day, he sprouts more meat and muscle, he walks taller, and after months of visits from the magical cat at his burial spot, he reaches out to Lisa the way she reached out to Frankie: like a dreamer. The stairs creak, and she stops fighting the house-cats trying to work their snouts between her hand and her organs. They jerk her left and right, and she ultimately collapses backward onto the bed. Her bleary gaze fixes on the entrance to the bedroom, where Charlie’s shadow shrinks to meet him. As he nears the bed with Frankie purring loudly in his arms, Lisa’s heart quickens. He sets the magic cat on the bed and cradles Lisa’s face as he leans over her. Frankie draws close too, his purr like a volcano of promise, but he doesn’t touch her. Not a nudge, not a sniff. “Why?” she croaks, and the cat blinks at Charlie, who nods in understanding. With a dreamy grin, he kisses Lisa's crown and hisses, “Non-essential.” He pats his leg as he walks out, and Frankie prances dutifully to his side. Lisa hears him purring after the door shuts, but when Biscuit burrows into her belly like he’s curling up in warm laundry, it sounds more like applause. In many ways, Norman Prentiss hardly needs an introduction. As a two-time Bram Stoker Award Winner, Norman is one of the strongest, most consistent voices in quiet horror today, beautifully carrying forth the legacy set by such luminaries as Charles L. Grant, T. M. Wright, and Robert Aickman. His stories seem so normal, even mundane at first, because he hides the blade well, and his deft slash is so subtle, you won't even realize you've been cut until you start bleeding. I don't think there's a writer today who captures the horror of emotional turmoil better than Norman, but by all means, please don't take my word for it. CABINET PEOPLE by Norman Prentiss M ira, I nearly got arrested yesterday. I had trouble explaining myself to the police, so be patient. It might not make sense to you either, at first. You know the layout of my building, with “u”- or horseshoe-shaped floors, identical doors lining both sides of each long hallway. Though it’s not exactly a maze, you’re not the only one who got lost the first time you visited. Part of the problem is that our building’s management doesn’t let us put decorations on our doors. And there’s no signage telling which way to turn for each range of numbers. Being cooped up for so long, I decided to take a walk outside yesterday, heading to the stairwell on the opposite part of the floor. From my end of the horseshoe, I walked until I turned one corner, another bowling-alley length until I rounded the next corner, and then I registered the Exit sign beyond the stretch of apartment doors. As I walked, I’d been worried I might run into somebody along the way, since I’d have to flatten myself against the wall to keep distance while they passed; then wondered about the wall itself—if anybody else had touched it, or if I’d touched it myself without thinking, to catch my balance, and the idea made me grab sanitizer gel from my pocket, squeeze out a few drops and spread it over my hands. I was mostly on automatic pilot when I reached the Exit door, turned the handle and shoulder-pushed my way into the stairwell. It was brighter than I remembered. Our hands-off management must have decided to replace a few bulbs, scare away the shadows and gray cement. They might have gone overboard, I thought: as if they’d broken through walls to add windows and opened curtains to admit natural sunlight. And where were the steps? I saw hardwood floors and black-and-white tiles, and no stairs heading up or down. It was my apartment. Without thinking, I’d turned around, must have subconsciously decided against the outside world for the day, piloted back to my own too-familiar dwelling. I washed my hands first thing, as we’re supposed to do. Counted to twenty, stared at the water coming out of the faucet while I tangled my fingers together. I think I laughed at myself, too. How funny that I’d changed my mind and walked back home without realizing it. My schedule’s so confused lately, sleeping at different hours, not driving to work each day—it’s no wonder my brain’s a little addled. The kitchen sink overlooks the open-concept dining area and the living room beyond. I still felt disoriented, and the arrangement of my furniture seemed wrong. I shook my hands dry, wiped them on the front of my trousers, then stepped around to the living area. My recliner was at the wrong angle and too far from the television. I grabbed it low down, lifted and pushed it back where it belonged. The chair’s legs scraped against the hardwood floor, and as I nudged the coffee table aside to make room an empty glass fell off the table and rolled under the flap of fabric along the bottom of the couch. The glass didn’t break, but it was doing that slow back and forth dance, a rhythmic pendulum.
